ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for fried black pomfret; the broader 'fried fish food product' class is used as the closest defensible ancestor anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pomfret, fried — mapped to FoodEx2 fish category with deep-frying preparation facet; closest defensible code for fried marine flatfish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for fried black pomfret (Parastromateus niger). Code A00LC (fish, fried) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor with preparation facet applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried black pomfret as a food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried black pomfret is one fillet weighing approximately 120 g, as commonly plated in Singaporean hawker centres and home meals.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Black Pomfret — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Black Pomfret (cr_id F03010016). All core macros, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, and dietary fibre are taken from this exact entry. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried pomfret (NDB 15236); sugar is confirmed negligible (0.0 g) given the near-zero carbohydrate content.

gi_source.title: N/A — Negligible carbohydrate content; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for fried black pomfret as carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.52 g per 100 g. No meaningful postprandial glucose response is expected. Per Veritas protocol for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 reflecting negligible GL. University of Sydney GI Database consulted and confirms no GI value exists for this food category, consistent with the not_applicable classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (171 mg/100 g) with high potassium (330 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Fried black pomfret has a low sodium content of 171 mg per 100 g and a high potassium content of 330 mg per 100 g, producing a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that supports blood pressure management; individuals with hypertension should avoid adding soy sauce, salt, or other high-sodium condiments and should prefer grilled or steamed preparation to limit additional fat and caloric load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.52 g/100 g) and very high protein (31.88 g/100 g) with no meaningful glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried black pomfret contains only 0.52 g of carbohydrate per 100 g and provides 31.88 g of high-quality protein, resulting in negligible glycaemic impact and supporting postprandial blood glucose stability; individuals with type 2 diabetes should be aware that deep-frying adds approximately 18 g of fat per 100 g which contributes excess calories and may worsen insulin resistance over time, and grilled or steamed pomfret is the preferred preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated fat content (18.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying, including estimated saturated fat (~4.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol (101 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While black pomfret is a lean marine fish with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids in its raw form, deep-frying substantially increases total fat to 18.2 g per 100 g with an estimated saturated fat of approximately 4.5 g per 100 g and cholesterol of 101 mg per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, avoid reused frying oil, and prefer steamed or grilled pomfret to preserve the cardioprotective omega-3 profile of the fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried black pomfret (炸黑鲳鱼; Ikan Bawal Hitam Goreng) is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian seafood dish made from black pomfret (Parastromateus niger), a prized marine flatfish with dark silvery skin, white flaky flesh, and a mild, slightly sweet flavour. The fish is typically cleaned, scored, marinated lightly with salt and sometimes turmeric or spices, then deep-fried in hot oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crisp while the interior remains tender and moist. Deep-frying transforms the naturally lean fish into a higher-calorie dish: per 100 g, it provides 293 kcal, 31.88 g of high-quality complete protein, 18.2 g of total fat (including approximately 4.5 g saturated fat), and only 0.52 g of carbohydrate, making it essentially a zero-carbohydrate, high-protein food. Sodium is moderate at 171 mg per 100 g and potassium is high at 330 mg per 100 g, supporting a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio. Calcium is notably high at 188 mg per 100 g, likely due to consumption of small bones. Cholesterol is 101 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is not applicable given negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, fried black pomfret is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes and hypertension when consumed in moderate portions without added high-sodium condiments, but the deep-frying preparation raises cardiovascular concern due to elevated total and saturated fat. Steamed or grilled preparation is recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis. It is a staple in Singaporean hawker centres, Chinese seafood restaurants, and home cooking, commonly served with steamed rice, sambal, or soy-ginger dipping sauce.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database exact entry for Fried Black Pomfret (cr_id F03010016), which is the highest-priority approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fried pomfret as HPB does not report this field; this is documented in notes. All other fields are completed per Veritas protocol.
notes: 1. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g (4.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried pomfret (NDB 15236) as the HPB Foodo database does not report saturated fat for this entry. This is an adapted value and is documented accordingly. 2. SUGAR: sugar_g is set to 0.0 g/100 g; this is confirmed negligible given total carbohydrate is only 0.52 g/100 g with no added sugar ingredients. Confirmed zero per HPB source context and USDA analogue. 3. DIETARY FIBRE: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100 g as reported by HPB; this is naturally zero for a pure fish product with no plant-based ingredients. 4. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient panels, and moisture data for fried black pomfret are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03010016) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation as of 2026. 5. GLYCEMIC INDEX: glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable because fried black pomfret contains only 0.52 g of carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content per Veritas protocol. Glycemic load per serving is reported as 0.0 to reflect negligible GL. 6. HALAL STATUS: Black pomfret is a scaled marine fish permissible under Islamic dietary law; halal status is set to true assuming no non-halal frying medium is used. 7. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI is mapped to the broad 'fried fish food product' class (closest_parent) as no species-specific URI for fried black pomfret exists. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest_parent defensible code for fried marine fish. SNOMED CT is omitted with honest gap note as no applicable food concept code was identified.
